charts.title: Topic Map
charts.description: Overview of the three non-monotonic logics, argumentation frameworks, preferences, and labelling rules covered in Week 2.

nodes.label:
Week 2
NMR & Argumentation
nodes.note: Week 2 (Modgil) presents three syntactic non-monotonic logics and shows how all of them can be unified under the argumentation framework view, where non-monotonic reasoning becomes a decision procedure between conflicting arguments.

nodes.label:
Logic
Programming
nodes.note: Prolog-style logic programming uses negation as failure NOT, where NOT X succeeds iff X cannot be proved. This makes the consequence relation non-monotonic.

nodes.label:
Maxiconsistent
Approaches
nodes.note: When a classical KB is inconsistent, the maxiconsistent approach picks preferred maximal consistent subsets ordered by stratification, then takes credulous or sceptical consequences.

nodes.label:
Default
Logic
nodes.note: Reiter-style default theories pair a consistent set W of facts with default rules that are applied when their antecedents hold and their consequents stay consistent.

nodes.label:
Argumentation
Frameworks
nodes.note: An argumentation framework is a directed graph of arguments connected by attacks. Dung 1995 showed it captures non-monotonic reasoning as a debate between conflicting arguments.

nodes.label: Preferences
nodes.note: When two arguments attack each other symmetrically, a preference ordering decides which attack succeeds. Preferences come from rule priorities, stratification, or goal orderings.

nodes.label:
Labelling
Rules
nodes.note: Each argument is labelled IN, OUT, or UNDEC according to three legal-labelling rules. Multiple legal labellings may exist for one framework.

nodes.label:
Negation
as Failure
nodes.note: NOT X holds whenever X is not provable from the current program. This is what makes adding new facts able to retract previous conclusions.

nodes.label:
Tweety
Trilogy
nodes.note: The Tweety knowledge base is extended three times: each addition either retracts or reinstates the conclusion that Tweety can fly, illustrating non-monotonicity.

nodes.label:
Stratified
KB
nodes.note: A KB is stratified into S1>S2>...>Sn equivalence classes by priority. The subclass principle (penguin rules over bird rules) is one common way to assign strata.

nodes.label:
Preferred
mcs
nodes.note: A preferred maximal consistent subset is built by starting with the highest stratum and adding lower-stratum formulas one at a time, keeping the set consistent.

nodes.label:
Default
Extensions
nodes.note: A default extension is a maximally consistent set obtained by closing W under classical consequence and the consequents of applicable defaults.

nodes.label:
Priority
over Defaults
nodes.note: Linear priorities over defaults ensure that higher-priority defaults are applied first. This typically reduces multiple extensions to a single preferred one.

nodes.label:
Dung 1995
Semantics
nodes.note: Dung 1995 introduced AFs along with conflict-free, admissible, preferred, grounded, stable and complete extension semantics, and the monotonic characteristic function F_AF.

nodes.label:
Credulous vs
Sceptical
nodes.note: Credulous consequences come from at least one preferred extension or labelling; sceptical consequences come from all of them. Grounded labelling is the sceptical one.

charts.title: Logic Programming with Negation as Failure
charts.description: Procedure for deciding whether a query Q is a non-monotonic consequence of a Prolog-style KB.

nodes.label: Query: KB |~ Q ?
nodes.note: We are given a logic programming KB and want to decide whether the query Q is a non-monotonic consequence under the negation-as-failure semantics.

nodes.label: Q a fact in KB?
nodes.note: Check whether Q appears directly as a ground fact in the program. Facts are immediately derivable without using any rule body.

nodes.label:
Find rule with
Q in head
nodes.note: Look up any rule whose head matches Q (after unification). The body of this rule will give the next subgoals that must be proved.

nodes.label:
Such a rule
exists?
nodes.note: If no rule has Q in its head and Q is not a fact, then Q cannot be derived under the SLDNF resolution strategy used by logic programs.

nodes.label:
Try to prove
each body literal
nodes.note: For each literal in the rule body, try to prove it. Positive literals are recursively queried; NOT-literals require the negation-as-failure subroutine.

nodes.label:
Body has
NOT X literal?
nodes.note: Decide whether the body contains a negation-as-failure literal NOT X. These literals must be handled differently from positive subgoals.

nodes.label:
X NOT provable
from KB?
nodes.note: Recursively query whether X can be proved from the current KB. If proving X fails finitely, NOT X succeeds; otherwise NOT X fails.

nodes.label:
All body literals
proved?
nodes.note: Check that every literal in the rule body has been successfully resolved (either positively or via successful negation as failure).

nodes.label: KB |~ Q
nodes.note: All body literals have been satisfied so the rule fires and Q is derivable. Q is a non-monotonic consequence of the program.

nodes.label: KB does NOT |~ Q
nodes.note: No applicable rule lets Q succeed. Q is not a non-monotonic consequence under negation-as-failure semantics.

charts.title: Constructing Preferred MCS from a Stratified KB
charts.description: Step-by-step procedure to build the preferred maximal consistent subsets of a stratified knowledge base.

nodes.label:
Stratified KB:
S1, S2, ..., Sn
nodes.note: We are given the KB partitioned into priority strata S1>S2>...>Sn. We will construct the preferred maximal consistent subsets one stratum at a time.

nodes.label: Compute mcs of S1
nodes.note: Find every maximal consistent subset of the highest-priority stratum S1. Each one will be the seed of a different preferred mcs.

nodes.label: Set i = 2
nodes.note: Initialise the stratum index. We will iteratively process strata 2 through n, extending the partial mcs from each previous stratum.

nodes.label: i <= n ?
nodes.note: Check whether more strata remain to be processed. If yes, extend the current partial mcs with the next stratum; otherwise we are done.

nodes.label:
Extend each partial mcs
with Si maximally
nodes.note: For every partial mcs E built so far, find every maximal subset of E union Si that is still consistent. This may split one partial mcs into several.

nodes.label: i = i + 1
nodes.note: Move to the next lower-priority stratum. Continue extending until all strata have been processed.

nodes.label:
Preferred mcs
E1, ..., Em
nodes.note: After all strata have been processed, the resulting consistent sets are the preferred mcs of the KB. Their classical consequences give credulous and sceptical NM consequences.

charts.title: Constructing a Default Extension
charts.description: Procedure for constructing a default extension of a default theory (W, Defaults) with optional priority ordering.

nodes.label:
Default theory
(W, Defaults)
nodes.note: We start from a consistent set W of classical facts and a set of default rules. Each default has antecedents and a consequent.

nodes.label: E := Cn(W)
nodes.note: Initialise the working extension E to the classical consequences of W. Defaults will be added one at a time as long as they remain consistent.

nodes.label:
Find applicable default
(highest priority first)
nodes.note: Identify a default whose antecedents are already in E. If priorities are given, choose the highest-priority such default; otherwise pick any one.

nodes.label:
Any applicable
default left?
nodes.note: Check whether any default not yet considered has antecedents that hold in the current extension. If none exist we are done.

nodes.label:
Consequent consistent
with E?
nodes.note: Test whether adding the default's consequent to E would still leave E classically consistent. Defaults can only fire if they preserve consistency.

nodes.label:
E := Cn(E union
{consequent})
nodes.note: Add the consequent to the extension and close under classical consequence. The default has now fired and is removed from consideration.

nodes.label: Skip this default
nodes.note: The default cannot fire because its consequent is inconsistent with the current extension. Mark it as inapplicable and continue.

nodes.label: Default extension E
nodes.note: No more defaults can be applied. The current E is a maximal consistent set closed under classical consequence and is therefore a default extension.

charts.title: Labelling Rules for Argumentation Frameworks
charts.description: Decision procedure for assigning IN, OUT, or UNDEC labels to an argument according to the three legal-labelling rules.

nodes.label: Argument x in AF
nodes.note: We are deciding the label of an argument x given the labels currently assigned to its attackers in the argumentation framework.

nodes.label:
x has any
attackers?
nodes.note: Check whether any other argument attacks x in the AF. Unattacked arguments are special: Rule 1 applies vacuously and they are always IN.

nodes.label:
Label x as IN
(unattacked)
nodes.note: x has no attackers, so all zero of its attackers are OUT vacuously. By Rule 1, x must be labelled IN.

nodes.label:
Some attacker
is IN?
nodes.note: Check whether at least one attacker of x has been labelled IN. If so, that successful attack defeats x by Rule 2.

nodes.label: Label x as OUT
nodes.note: At least one attacker of x is IN, so by Rule 2 the argument x must be labelled OUT - it is defeated by a winning attacker.

nodes.label:
All attackers
are OUT?
nodes.note: Check whether every attacker of x has been labelled OUT. If yes then x is acceptable and Rule 1 applies non-vacuously.

nodes.label: Label x as IN
nodes.note: Every attacker of x is OUT, so x is defended against all its attackers. By Rule 1 the argument x must be labelled IN.

nodes.label: Label x as UNDEC
nodes.note: No attacker of x is IN but at least one is UNDEC. By Rule 3, x must be labelled UNDEC - the argument is in an unresolved cycle of doubt.

charts.title: Three Non-monotonic Logics - Comparison
charts.description: Taxonomy of the three non-monotonic logics covered in Week 2 with their key constructs.

nodes.label: NM Logics
nodes.note: All three logics share the property that adding new information can retract previous conclusions, but they differ in how the KB is structured and how consequences are computed.

nodes.label:
Logic
Programming
nodes.note: Prolog-style programs with rules and facts. The non-monotonic flavour comes from negation as failure.

nodes.label: Maxiconsistent
nodes.note: Classical KB possibly inconsistent, partitioned into priority strata. Reasoning is over preferred maximal consistent subsets.

nodes.label: Default Logic
nodes.note: Reiter-style theories with classical facts W and a set of default rules. Consequences are over default extensions.

nodes.label:
NOT (negation
as failure)
nodes.note: Negation as failure: NOT X succeeds whenever X cannot be proved from the program. This is what introduces non-monotonicity.

nodes.label:
Single relation
KB |~ Q
nodes.note: There is a single notion of consequence: KB |~ Q iff the Prolog query ?Q returns true under the SLDNF resolution strategy.

nodes.label: Preferred mcs
nodes.note: A maximal subset of the KB built by extending strata top-down, keeping consistency at every stage.

nodes.label:
Credulous /
Sceptical
nodes.note: Credulous consequences hold in some preferred mcs; sceptical consequences hold in all of them. Bottom is never credulous.

nodes.label: Default extension
nodes.note: A maximally consistent set built by closing W under defaults, where each default fires only if its consequent is consistent with the current extension.

nodes.label:
Priority over
defaults
nodes.note: A linear ordering on defaults selects which to apply first. Higher-priority defaults block conflicting lower-priority ones.
